attributes.stem: A 38-year-old man with type 2 diabetes mellitus is reviewed by his GP for ongoing management. His most recent HbA1c was 8.5%. His current BMI is 34 kg/m2 and despite weight loss attempts over the last few years, his BMI is unchanged from when he was first diagnosed with diabetes 3 years ago. His only current medication is metformin and his GP is keen to offer another agent that will not only help his glycaemic control, but also assist with weight loss.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following would be the most appropriate addition to his current drug regimen?
attributes.explanation:
The correct answer to the above stem is Semaglutide, a glucagon-like peptide-1 (GLP-1) agonist. Current Australian guidelines and practice favour Semaglutide as a choice of agent for patients with diabetes and comorbid severe obesity. GLP-1 and glucose-dependent insulinotropic polypeptide (GIP) are both incretin hormones inactivated by dipeptidyl peptidase-4 (DPP-4) which act by stimulating insulin secretion after an oral glucose load (notably, they do not act following an intravenous glucose load). Thus, utilisation of pharmacological levels of GLP-1 can revive the insulin excretion process which is insufficient in type 2 diabetes mellitus (due to insulin resistance +/- insulin deficiency relative to demand). GLP-1RA reduce blood glucose excursions by 3 mechanisms: enhancing glucose-dependent insulin release from beta-cells, slowing gastric emptying and reducing appetite. GLP-1 agonists have the additional benefit of promoting weight loss with one study exhibiting 16.0% body weight loss when compared to 5.7% loss in placebo group when both groups were on a low calorie diet over 68 weeks.

Empagliflozin belongs to a class of oral anti-hyperglycaemic agents called SGLT-2 inhibitors. These medications are recommended to improve glycaemic control in patients who have not experienced sufficient HbA1c improvement with Metformin alone. This class works by reducing glucose uptake in the proximal convoluted tubule of the kidneys. This mechanism of action has the effect of creating a glucosuria. Urinary glucose excretion has been linked with mild weight loss through a combination of caloric loss via glucosuria and water loss via osmotic diuresis. The weight loss associated with this class of medication is most pronounced in the initial phases of therapy. The side effects of this SGLT-2 inhibitors include euglycaemic diabetic ketoacidosis (DKA), increased urinary frequency and an increased risk of genitourinary infections. 

In Australia, the Pharmaceutical Benefits Scheme (PBS) subsidises one, but not both of, GLP-1RA and SGLT-2i with metformin therapy. To access GLP-1RA on the PBS, SGLT-2i therapy must be trialled with ineffective improvement in glycaemic control, or contraindicated, before it can be prescribed under this scheme.

Gliclazide is a sulphonylurea drug, which helps stimulate insulin release from the pancreatic beta cells. Gliclazide can be associated with weight gain, rather than weight loss. As such, gliclazide is an incorrect answer. 

Pre-mixed insulin is not the correct answer as insulin is associated with weight gain rather than weight loss. Insulin is a suitable choice in type 2 diabetes mellitus, but only if the patient has an excessively high HbA1c that is unlikely to be brought under control by oral agents, or if the patient’s glycaemic control is inadequate despite multiple oral antihyperglycemic agents. In this case, it would be inappropriate to introduce insulin as the patient has only previously been on metformin and is yet to trial additional oral agents. Furthermore, his HbA1c of 8.5% is not high enough to consider insulin. 

Pioglitazone belongs to the thiazolidinedione class and works by increasing insulin sensitivity. This answer is incorrect for two reasons: (1) thiazolidinediones can be associated with fluid retention and weight gain, rather than weight loss; and (2) this drug class has largely been superseded by the newer oral agents (e.g. GLP-1 agonists, SGLT-2 inhibitors, DPP-IV inhibitors etc). Therefore it would be inappropriate to introduce pioglitazone to this patient.
learningPoints: GLP-1 receptor agonists (with the exception of Exanatide) are the agents of choice for type-2 diabetes mellitus and comorbid severe obesity
references: Andrianesis V, Glykofridi S, Doupis J. The renal effects of SGLT2 inhibitors and a mini-review of the literature. Ther Adv in Endocrinol Metab. 2016 Dec;7(5-6):212–28.
Lee PC, Ganguly S, Goh S‐. Weight loss associated with sodium‐glucose cotransporter‐2 inhibition: a review of evidence and underlying mechanisms. Obesity reviews. 2018 Dec;19(12):1630–41

Collins L, Costello RA. Glucagon-like Peptide-1 Receptor Agonists. [Updated 2022 May 29]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2022 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK551568/

Wadden TA, Bailey TS, Billings LK, Davies M, Frias JP, Koroleva A, Lingvay I, O'Neil PM, Rubino DM, Skovgaard D, Wallenstein SOR, Garvey WT; STEP 3 Investigators. Effect of Subcutaneous Semaglutide vs Placebo as an Adjunct to Intensive Behavioral Therapy on Body Weight in Adults With Overweight or Obesity: The STEP 3 Randomized Clinical Trial. JAMA. 2021 Apr 13;325(14):1403-1413. doi: 10.1001/jama.2021.1831. PMID: 33625476; PMCID: PMC7905697.
